Spatial & multi-omic profiling of ovarian cancer to identify immune vulnerabilities for precision immunotherapy

18.06.2025


Aleksandra Shabanova
Doctoral researcher
University of Helsinki, Finland

High-grade serous ovarian cancer (HGSC)

2

50%
Of patients

5-year mortality rate

Current treatment &
 associated tumor samples

Primary debulking surgery

Neoadjuvant chemotherapy followed by Interval debulking surgery

Post-chemotherapy

Pre-chemotherapy

Speaker notes:
high-grade serous cancer (HGSC), is the most common and aggresive subtype of ovarian cancer.
The treatment approaches for the initial management of newly diagnosed ovarian cancer encompass two main paradigms. The first option is primary debulking surgery (PDS) followed by combination platinum-based chemotherapy. On the other hand, when PDS could be challenging to perform, neoadjuvant chemotherapy (NACT) is administered first. Next, it is followed by interval debulking surgery (IDS) and further chemotherapy after surgery.
However, currently, patients has 50% have 5 year mortality rate.

https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/30813239/

Tumor microenviroment (TME) impact on cancer immunity & response to immunotherapies

Subset of patients have immunereactive subtype with tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes & ativated signaling pathways associated with immune cells

Minimal reported response to PD1 or PDL1 blockade

Adapted from Ghisoni et al. (2024)

Mellman et al. (2023)

~50%
Of patients

tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes in intraepithelium associated with improved survival

Hwang et al. (2011) & Zhang et al. (2003)

3

Trial name/ref. | Treatment | Overall response rate (%)
JAVELIN Solid Tumour | Avelumab | 9.6
Hamanishi et al. | Nivolumab | 15
KEYNOTE-028 | Pembrolizumab | 11.5

Speaker notes:
Around 50% of epithelial ovarian cancer patients exhibit intraepithelial tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes (TILs), which correlate with prolonged survival and suggest a presence of immunoreactive subset. Because immune checkpoint blockade (e.g., PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitors) depends on preexisting TILs, some patients should respond; however, clinical trials have shown limited efficacy, likely due to the absence of reliable biomarkers to identify responders.

A single biomarker is insufficient to predict benefit from immune checkpoint inhibitors in ovarian cancer, as whole tumor microenvironment (TME) was shown to play a critical role in regulating cancer immunity.

cient to predict benefit from immune checkpoint inhibitors in ovarian cancer, as whole tumor microenvironment (TME) was shown to play a critical role in regulating cancer immunity. Therefore, a comprehensive assessment of TME—including immune cells’ distribution, subsets, functionality, and their interactions with other cells—is essential for improving patient selection and therapeutic outcomes.

t-CycIF & GeoMx for spatial exploration of TME

tCycIF multiplex imunofluorescent image

tCycIF

GeoMx

Pan-CK IBA1 CD8

DNA Pan-CK CD45

Multiplexed immunofluorescence

Spatial transcriptomics

4

Speaker notes:
spatial profiling methodologies can systematically capture and illuminate the physical localization of the diverse components within the TME
One notable approach is t-CyCIF, a cyclic microscopy method allowing multiplexed imaging of proteins within the TME, resulting in final images with multiple channels – protein targets.
On the other hand, we have spatial transcriptomic approach GeoMx that allows to elucidate spatial molecular programs at whole transcriptome information for selected region of interest (ROIs). 

In our project, we are attemping to combine both approaches

Integration of tCycIF and GeoMx for spatial molecular profiling

GeoMx

tCycIF

5

FFPE
sample

2 adjacent slides

Speaker notes:
We do it the following way: First, tCycIF is used to collect whole slide images on adjacent to GeoMx slide. These images guide our ROI selection for GeoMx, where two scans are overlay and selected ROIs are subjected for whole transcriptome analysis.
For this projct, we created a semi-automatised ROI selection tool

Representation of whole-slide image at megapixel resolution

Semi-automatised ROI selection allows immune hub identification across whole slide images

6

Speaker notes:
The tool has 3 key steps:
It preprocesses the selected channels to identify the immune hubs. Here, as examples, we have CD4 and CD8 channels, where the final mask image shows poteantial immune hubs of corresponding cell type.
The tool identifies the overlapping hubs between selected channels and proposes candidate ROIs based on the size
Finally, human is required to inspect candidate ROIs and select final tentative ROIs

Identification of immune hubs and their molecular programs

5 markers

CD4 (CD4 T-cells)
CD8 (CD8 T-cells)
CD11c (DCs)
Iba1 (macrophages)
NKG2a_1 (NKs)

Identification of 
close interactions between 5 immune cell types in stroma and tumor

7

GeoMx ROI

Speaker notes:
We selected 5 immune cells: CD4, CD8 T cells, DCs, macrophages and NKs to study their hubs and interactions in all possible and present combinations. We focused on both stroma, tumor and TSI aggregates.

Unique cohort of paired samples: pre and post chemotherapy

9 chemo-treated samples

4 paired samples

10 paired samples

18 chemo-treated samples

8

210 QC segments

1048 segments

Speaker notes:
To study these immune cell inetractions, we used a unique cohort of 14 paired samples, where we had both pre-chemo and post-chemotherapy sample subjected for multi-omic profiling. We also collected 27 unpaired chemo-treated samples. In total, we have QC already 210 tumor and stroma segments, with ~1000 segments in progress.

Diverse Immune interaction hubs revealed by tCycIF & GeoMx

Hubbing of immune cells with themselves
CD11c + Iba1 CD4 T cells + Iba1 + CD11c + CD8

Spatial proximity of cells in whole slide tCycIF

Collected hubs in GeoMx

Interaction enrichment by Sample

Fold change between counts of A & B neighbours vs counts of A interacting with neither A nor B and B interacting with neither A nor B

Speaker notes:
We’re seeing early signs of immune cell clustering and DC–T cell interactions in our first batch tCycIF images. But what’s striking is how sample-dependent these patterns are — something also captured in the GeoMx interaction hubs.
This points to a diverse and complex TIME across patients, emphasizing the need for deeper multi-modal analysis of both spatial TME features and associated patient data

9

Next steps

SPACEstat package

Identification of underlying mechanisms in immune microenviroment

Discovery of clinically relevant interactions

Integration with clinicomolecular patient data

Data consolidation for identification of 
immune microenviromment mechanisms & interactions

Cell-level analysis at TME compartments
Delaunay network analysis of immune cell hubs

12

Speaker notes:
As a next step, we will explore the immune microenvironment by analyzing immune networks and their spatial interactions.
Then, we will integrate single-cell t-CyCIF and mini-bulk GeoMx data by representing the immune landscape as a network of interacting nodes, where selected regions of interest (ROIs) are covered by GeoMx profiling.
This network framework will then be enriched with available clinico-molecular patient data to uncover mechanisms of immune regulation within the tumor microenvironment—ultimately aiming to identify novel biomarkers and therapeutic targets.

Thank you for your attention!
